channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Ukraine Has a Plan to Build Back Better
item.description: The war-torn country wants to reconstruct in a way that is environmentally, socially, and geopolitically more sustainable.

item.title: Iran Says Strait of Hormuz Is ‘Completely Open’
item.description: But it’s unclear whether the strategic waterway is really open without conditions.

item.title: Hormuz is (apparently) unblocked. Energy markets remain a mess
item.description: Mines, mistrust and missing ships will keep markets tight for months

item.title: Order Without Order
item.description: Our fixation with defining the emerging global order hides the true complexity of our neo-medieval moment.

item.title: What in the World?
item.description: Test yourself on the week of April 11: Hungary votes, Trump insults the pope, and the war in Sudan enters its fourth year.

item.title: Cover Story newsletter: The Mythos moment
item.description: An exclusive look at how we designed our cover

item.title: Did London’s Dirty Money Really Kill a Teenage Fantasist?
item.description: Patrick Radden Keefe’s “London Falling” is a mystery that turns into a tragedy.

item.title: The Iran War Comes for the ‘King of Chemicals’
item.description: The conflict is wreaking havoc on an obscure sector that is more important than you’d think.

item.title: Is bone broth good for you?
item.description: Or is it just tasty?

item.title: South American Crime Groups Are Going for Gold
item.description: Why exports of illegally sourced gold are gaining on drugs in funding the continent’s illicit economy.

item.title: The World Needs an Oil Buyers’ Club
item.description: As the world is plunged into another energy crisis, market allocation is leading to grossly unjust outcomes, as the rich outbid the poor. A multilateral oil buyers' club is urgently needed to defend a price ceiling in global oil markets and allocate resources in a way that meets people’s essential needs and minimizes the economic fallout.

item.title: A New Security Architecture for the Middle East
item.description: The tense negotiations between the United States and Iran have exposed the limits of bilateral diplomacy. With the crisis fueled by overlapping, interconnected conflicts, the only viable path forward is a broader regional framework that addresses the Strait of Hormuz, nuclear proliferation, Palestinian statehood, and proxy warfare.

item.title: Japan’s Constitutional Theater: Revising Article 9 Would Be a Mistake
item.description: A constitutional revision solves the wrong problem – and creates new complications.

item.title: The Geopolitical Importance of India’s Shrinking ‘Red Corridor’
item.description: India has turned the corner in its battle against Naxalism – and effectively governing its own margins is an essential step toward global power status.

item.title: China Was Once Buying Up Sri Lankan Ports. Now It’s India’s Turn.
item.description: The Indian Ocean has no shortage of distressed strategic assets: financially stressed yards, ports, and logistics infrastructure in small states that cannot sustain them independently.

item.title: Move Over, Hungary: Spain Is China’s New Best Friend in the EU
item.description: With Viktor Orban’s election loss, Pedro Sanchez is now Beijing’s most useful European leader.

item.title: Fossil-Fuel Investments Are a Fiduciary Risk
item.description: The Iran war has reminded everyone, but especially Africans, of the structural instability of fossil-fuel prices. For African trustees, directors, asset managers, and other fiduciaries, the question is not whether capital should reposition, but whether institutions will act before events compel them to do so.

item.title: Japan’s Takaichi to Forge Closer Cooperation With Australia in Rare Earths
item.description: In an era defined by geopolitical fragmentation, the Australia-Japan partnership shows how middle powers can cooperate to mitigate risk and enhance resilience.

item.title: To Strengthen Climate Resilience, Focus on Social Protection
item.description: The international community is increasingly trying to distinguish between climate, development, and humanitarian finance—as if they can be neatly compartmentalized. But this siloed approach overlooks how social-protection programs providing cash transfers to vulnerable households can strengthen resilience to climate shocks.

item.title: A lasting Lebanese peace still looks a long way off
item.description: Donald Trump has enforced a ceasefire, but that is just a start

item.title: Europe’s Digital Decade Is in Disarray
item.description: Under the banner of the European Union’s Digital Decade agenda, Europe is investing in digitalization to protect its industries from shocks like the one currently emanating from the Middle East. But if EU leaders think their current program is sufficient, they are in for a rude awakening.

item.title: A European Populist Defends an American Pope
item.description: Italian Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni's response to Donald Trump's attacks on Pope Leo XIV may strain her relationship with the White House, but it is likely to help her domestically. Remarkably, this outcome materialized in a staunch, if unwitting, defense of European values.

item.title: The Origins and Fate of Digital Sovereignty
item.description: Among the world’s major economies, only China and Russia have managed to build digital industries that stand apart from American platforms. Once foreign incumbents reach critical mass, local firms have little room to scale, leaving most countries without a viable path to technological autonomy.

item.title: The Uncertain Future of the China-Myanmar Economic Corridor
item.description: Despite the swearing-in of a new "civilian" government, progress on the project is likely to remain sluggish.

item.title: Vietnam’s Top Leader Concludes 4-Day State Visit to China
item.description: Meeting in Beijing, To Lam and Xi Jinping declared that they view the bilateral relationship "as a strategic choice of overarching and long-term significance.”

item.title: America and Iran’s Long Road to Peace
item.description: A grand bargain is out of reach, but a comprehensive deal is possible.

item.title: Europe Still Needs China
item.description: Washington, not Beijing, is the bigger threat.

item.title: The Iran War Is a Win for China
item.description: At a meeting with Xi next month, Trump will be on the backfoot.

item.title: The Iran War’s Impact on India and Pakistan
item.description: The economic brunt of a prolonged war would be borne by the Indian and Pakistani people, who have no part in it but no escape from its consequences.

item.title: Plugging into Reality: The ASEAN Power Grid
item.description: For decades, the Southeast Asian bloc has envisioned the creation of a region-spanning power grid. Is the project finally set for take-off?

item.title: Australian PM Secures Fuel, Fertilizer Supplies During Visits to Malaysia and Brunei
item.description: During his quick visits to the two nations, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese and his hosts pledged to “strengthen energy supply chain resilience.”

item.title: Uzbekistan Wants Nuclear Energy, But Can It Afford the Water Cost?
item.description: The country is betting on nuclear power to secure its energy future – but it is doing so in one of the most water-stressed regions in the world.

item.title: How China’s Arctic Ambitions Inflate Russia’s Geopolitical Leverage
item.description: Russia’s law suggests that Moscow is not preparing the Northern Sea Route for international use. It may instead be profiting from a misplaced expectation.
